You map the proxy activity against your contact network — two relationships are directly threatened by the Houthi escalation. Damage control first, then recalibration.

Iranian proxies are escalating and your Gulf contacts are spooked. MBS views proxy threats as existential — the Houthi campaign hit his airports, his refineries, his sense of security. You need to demonstrate that your framework addresses proxies, not just the strait. Otherwise the trust erodes from the edges in.
highEscalation: The escalation report reads like a term sheet for a deal nobody wants. Strikes exchanged, forces committed, sunk costs accumulating on both sides. You've seen deals die this way — when both parties have invested too much to back down. The off-ramp needs to give Tehran a way to de-escalate without looking like they lost. You start drafting.

Every contact in the network called overnight. Not to share intelligence — to ask whether you can still deliver a resolution. High escalation tests trust like nothing else. You spend the early morning on calls, reinforcing commitments, managing expectations, quietly reminding partners that the framework holds even under fire.
straitOpening: Oil is flowing and the deal pipeline is flowing with it. Every open day builds trust, trust builds frameworks, and frameworks build lasting architecture. You review the normalization timeline against the strait metrics — the convergence is remarkable. If this holds for fourteen days, you've built something that outlasts this crisis.

The strait is open and the Gulf network is humming with cautious optimism. You spend the morning updating the relationship matrix — trust levels ticking up, exposure metrics ticking down. This is what the architecture was designed to produce. Now the question is whether you can lock it in before the next disruption.
default: Morning espresso, overnight cables, contact trust dashboard review. The standard routine of a back-channel operator in a forward position. Nothing dramatic in the overnight reporting, which gives you space to work the relationship pipeline — three outreach calls scheduled before the formal briefing, each one carefully calibrated.

You review the morning intelligence summary the way an investor reviews a portfolio — scanning for risk, opportunity, and correlation. The strait situation is unchanged, which in deal-making terms means the table is set. Time to decide which relationship to invest in today.

highTension: You pull up the dashboard and the tension graph looks like your viewer count during a world-first raid — straight vertical. Chat is going absolutely insane. The Discord is pinging every three seconds. This is peak content and also, apparently, peak global crisis. The disconnect between your gaming chair and the nuclear submarine tracking screen has never felt more surreal.

Reddit's front page is wall-to-wall strait content and half the comments are tagging you. Tension is through the roof and the engagement metrics are unprecedented. You take a sip of your drink and stare at the situation room feed on your monitor. This is the most unhinged content arc imaginable and somehow you're in charge of it.
calmDay: Quiet day. Chat's gonna get bored. You scroll through the overnight analytics — nothing popped, no incidents, no drama. The strait is calm and so is the content. You could use this time to theory-craft, build some longer-term plays, but the audience wants action. 'ResidentSleeper' is already appearing in chat.

You pull up the feeds and there's nothing. No explosions, no seizures, no Iranian fast boats doing anything interesting. The numbers are flat and so is the content. You lean back in the chair and think about what play to make when the world isn't generating content for you. Time to manufacture some.
lowApproval: The viewer count is dropping and it's not because of your content schedule. Low approval means the normies are turning on you — the casual audience that doesn't understand the meta. Chat's split between 'trust the process' and 'uninstall.' You've rebuilt communities before but this feels different when the stakes are real.

You check the analytics and the approval graph looks like a sub count after a bad take. The community is fracturing and the Reddit threads have turned hostile. Some of it is bot-driven, some of it is real. Either way, the audience is telling you something and you need to listen before the bleed-out is permanent.
budgetCrisis: The budget screen looks like a raid wipe — everything's in red and the resources are gone. You've speedrun games with no gold before but this is different. Every action costs real money you don't have and there's no farming route to grind it back. Time to min-max harder than you've ever min-maxed anything.

You pull up the budget and start doing the math out loud, the way you do on stream when you're theory-crafting a build. The numbers don't work. No matter how you optimize, you're spending more than you have. Chat would call this a soft-lock. The question is whether you can sequence-break your way out.
lowOilFlow: Oil flow is in the gutter and it's the kind of stat that affects everything downstream. You pull up the economic cascade model — it's like watching a chain pull in a raid where one missed mechanic wipes the whole group. Gas prices, supply chains, allied confidence — everything flows from the strait and the strait is barely flowing.

You're staring at the oil flow graph and it looks like a speedrun that hit a game-breaking bug. The number is way too low, the economy is hemorrhaging, and every minute it stays like this compounds the damage. Chat's spamming 'F' and they're not entirely wrong. This needs a fix and it needs one now.
diplomaticProgress: The diplomacy stats are actually going up, which feels like when a run suddenly starts going well after hours of resets. You don't trust it — good RNG always feels like it's about to end. But the numbers are real: tension down, diplomatic capital up, actual progress. Chat's cautiously optimistic, which for chat is basically euphoria.

Diplomatic track is producing results and the analytics confirm it — the engagement is high but the tone has shifted from 'we're all gonna die' to 'actually this might work.' You've seen enough comeback arcs to know this is the dangerous part. The audience expects a twist. Don't give them one.
proxyThreats: The proxy threat map looks like a multiplayer lobby where everyone picked the same faction against you. Houthis, Hezbollah, Iraqi militias — Iran basically opened a second, third, and fourth front and none of them have their name on it. Chat's trying to explain asymmetric warfare in Twitch terms and honestly they're not wrong.

You pull up the proxy report and it reads like patch notes for the worst update ever. Multiple Iranian-backed groups escalating simultaneously, coordinated timing, no direct attribution. It's the geopolitical equivalent of a multi-boxer griefing your server. You need to figure out if you hit the alts or go after the main.
highEscalation: The escalation level on the dashboard has a color you haven't seen before and it's not green. Strikes have happened. Real strikes, on real targets, with real consequences. Chat went from meme-posting to dead silent in about thirty seconds. This is the part of the run where one wrong input ends everything.

You're looking at the escalation brief and your hands are doing the thing they do before a difficult boss pull — slight tremor, hyper-focus. The game has shifted from strategy to execution. Mistakes at this escalation level don't have respawns. Chat knows it. You know it. The engagement metrics are through the roof but nobody's laughing.
straitOpening: The strait metrics are green and trending greener. Oil flowing, shipping moving, days ticking up toward the win condition. You've watched enough progress bars to know the last 10% takes the longest, but the math is working. Chat is counting down with you. If you hit fourteen days, the clip will break the internet.

The run is going well. Too well, maybe — you've been burned by false hope before, but the numbers don't lie. Strait open, oil flowing, win condition approaching. The community is hype. The content is hype. You just need to not throw it in the final stretch, which historically is exactly when you throw it.
default: You settle into the chair, pull up the feeds, and start scanning. Nothing dramatic, nothing catastrophic, nothing that's going to generate a clip by itself. Just another day of reading reports, checking metrics, and trying to figure out the optimal play. The content isn't making itself today — you'll have to make it.

Morning feeds are up. The situation is what it is — not great, not terrible, just the ongoing grind of a crisis that doesn't have a clear meta. You check chat sentiment, review the analytics, and start planning moves. The audience is watching. They always are. Time to give them something worth watching.

highTension: The tension numbers are screaming and the Telegram channels are on fire. Your people see it clearly — decades of interventionist foreign policy created this exact crisis, and now the establishment wants to solve it with more intervention. The base is watching to see if you fold under pressure or stand firm. You won't fold.

You pull up the briefing and the tension is exactly where the war hawks want it — high enough to justify escalation, high enough to make withdrawal look weak. That's the trap. The establishment has been building this trap for years. Your base didn't send you here to walk into it. They sent you here to burn it down.
calmDay: Quiet morning. The regime wants you to think quiet means safe, but you know better. Quiet means they're planning. The establishment uses calm days to consolidate, to move pieces into position, to set the table for the next manufactured crisis. You use them to build. Rally planning, messaging strategy, base activation.

The briefing is calm. Good. Calm days are when you do the real work — the work the cameras don't see. Base organizing, Telegram strategy, building the parallel communication structure that bypasses the establishment media. Every quiet day is a gift for movement-building. Use it.
lowApproval: The approval numbers are a lie. You know it, the base knows it, and deep down the pollsters know it. They oversample the coasts, they weight for demographics that don't reflect real America, and they publish numbers designed to demoralize. The rallies tell the real story. The Telegram engagement tells the real story.

Low approval is the establishment's weapon. They manufacture consent through polls, then use the polls to justify removing you. You've seen this playbook before — they ran it on every America First candidate who got too close to power. The base isn't demoralized. The base is angry. Good. Angry people show up.
budgetCrisis: The budget is bleeding out and every dollar is going overseas. This is exactly what you campaigned against — American treasure poured into foreign adventures while American cities crumble. The budget crisis isn't a policy failure. It's the inevitable result of empire, and the only solution is contraction. Bring the money home.

Every line item in the budget report is proof. Carrier operations: billions. Intelligence operations: billions. Diplomatic missions: billions. And for what? To keep the strait open for oil that mostly goes to China and Europe. The base sees these numbers. The base knows who's paying. And the base is done paying.
lowOilFlow: Oil flow is collapsing and the establishment is panicking because their entire world order runs on cheap Persian Gulf crude. But you've been saying it for years: energy independence. Drill at home. Pipeline at home. The strait shouldn't matter to Americans and the fact that it does is a policy choice the globalists made, not an inevitability.

The oil crisis is the establishment's crisis, not yours. Real Americans understand that dependence on Middle Eastern oil is a chain they put around our necks, and the solution isn't more gunboats in the strait — it's domestic production. Every barrel that doesn't flow through Hormuz is a barrel that proves the America First case.
diplomaticProgress: The diplomats are celebrating their 'progress' and you're skeptical. You've seen these deals before — they give away American leverage, they commit American resources, and they dissolve the moment the cameras turn off. Real progress means bringing troops home, not signing frameworks that keep them deployed.

The diplomatic track is producing what the establishment calls results. The base calls it something else: entanglement. Every framework, every protocol, every multilateral agreement is another thread tying America to a region where it doesn't belong. Diplomacy that leads to withdrawal is progress. Everything else is a trap.
proxyThreats: Iranian proxies are hitting across five countries and the war hawks are using every attack to argue for deeper intervention. This is the strategy — create enemies everywhere so the military-industrial complex always has a justification. The proxies exist because America is in the region. Remove America, remove the target.

The proxy threat briefing is the establishment's favorite document because it makes the case for permanent presence. Houthis in Yemen, militias in Iraq, Hezbollah in Lebanon — an endless enemies list that requires an endless military commitment. Your base sees through it. The question is whether you can make the country see through it too.
highEscalation: The escalation report confirms everything the movement warned about. This is where interventionism leads — always, inevitably, to the brink of war. The establishment marched America to this cliff and now they want you to jump. The base is watching. The country is watching. This is the moment that defines whether America First means something.

Strikes have happened. American weapons hitting Iranian targets, Iranian weapons hitting American interests. This is what the neocons wanted all along — a hot war in the Gulf that justifies the next twenty years of military spending and foreign entanglement. You have a choice: be their war president, or be the one who stops it.
straitOpening: The strait is open and oil is flowing, which the establishment will claim as vindication of their strategy. Don't let them rewrite history. The crisis happened because America was over-committed in the region. The resolution should be withdrawal, not entrenchment. Winning means coming home, not staying forever.

Oil flow is up and the win condition is approaching, but winning means different things to different people. The establishment wants to win and stay. America First means winning and leaving. Every open day should bring troops one step closer to home, not cement another decade of presence.
default: Another morning in the machine. You scan the briefing while your team preps the day's messaging. The situation in the strait is what it is — a crisis manufactured by decades of interventionism, managed by an establishment that profits from it. Your job is to break the cycle. The base is counting on it.

You pull up the morning briefing alongside the Telegram analytics. The establishment gives you their version of reality; the base gives you theirs. The truth is somewhere in between but closer to the base than the briefers would ever admit. Time to figure out today's play and make sure the movement stays ahead of the narrative.
